hypotheses: Relaxation is hypothesized to be positively associated with joviality (H1a), serenity (H1b), and self-assurance (H1c), and negatively associated with hostility (H1d), fear (H1e), sadness (H1f), and fatigue (H1g).
Mastery experiences are expected to be positively correlated with joviality (H2a), serenity (H2b), and self-assurance (H2c).
Control during the weekend is proposed to be positively linked to joviality (H3a), serenity (H3b), and self-assurance (H3c).

Psychological detachment during the weekend is associated with higher positive affect (joviality, serenity, self-assurance) and lower negative affect (sadness, hostility, fear, fatigue), though effects may vary by timing.
Non-work hassles during the weekend are hypothesized to increase negative affective states (hostility, fear, sadness, fatigue).
claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction / Hypotheses section of the paper (hypotheses described in the paper's hypotheses section; specific page not stated in summary).
claim.statement: This study focuses on the relationship between weekend recovery experiences (relaxation, mastery, control, psychological detachment, and non-work hassles) and affective states during the following workweek.
claim.statement_location: Abstract and Introduction (aims and main claim stated at the start of the paper; specific page not stated in summary).

data.source: Self-report surveys/questionnaires administered to participants (including the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule - PANAS-X and the Recovery Experience Questionnaire).
data.wave_or_subset: Three measurement occasions: Time 1 (Friday before the weekend), Time 2 (Sunday evening/end of weekend), Time 3 (following Friday/end of workweek).
data.notes: Measures included PANAS-X for affect and the Recovery Experience Questionnaire for weekend experiences. Confirmatory factor analysis validated four distinct weekend experience constructs. Data are self-report; limitations include possible common method bias and measurement timing (weekend experiences and Time 2 affect assessed at same time).

method.description: A longitudinal survey study with preschool teachers (N=229) measuring weekend recovery experiences and affective states at three time points. Confirmatory factor analyses tested factor structure of affect and recovery constructs. Hierarchical regression analyses predicted Time 2 (end-of-weekend) and Time 3 (end-of-following-workweek) affective states from weekend experiences while controlling for baseline affect (Time 1) and demographics.
steps: Administer baseline survey on Friday (Time 1) measuring affect and control variables/demographics.
Administer survey on Sunday evening (Time 2) measuring weekend recovery experiences (relaxation, mastery, control, detachment) and non-work hassles, and affect at end of weekend.
Administer follow-up survey the following Friday (Time 3) measuring affect at end of the following workweek.
Conduct confirmatory factor analyses to validate factor structure (seven-factor model for affect; four-factor model for recovery experiences).
Compute hierarchical regression analyses predicting Time 2 and Time 3 affective states from weekend experiences, controlling for Time 1 affect and demographic covariates.
Report regression coefficients, model R-squared values, and model fit indices; interpret effects and draw conclusions.
method.models: Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) for factor validation; hierarchical multiple regression analyses predicting affect at Time 2 and Time 3 controlling for baseline affect and demographics. Model comparisons (seven-factor vs two-factor) using chi-square difference tests were also conducted.
method.outcome_variable: Affective states (seven discrete affective dimensions: four negative - hostility, sadness, fear, fatigue; three positive - joviality, self-assurance, serenity) measured at Time 2 and Time 3.
method.independent_variables: Weekend recovery experiences: relaxation, mastery, control, psychological detachment; and non-work hassles.
method.control_variables: Baseline affective states at Time 1 and demographic variables (specific demographics not detailed in summary).

results.summary: Weekend recovery experiences explained significant variance in affect at the end of the weekend and, to a lesser extent, in affect during the following workweek. Relaxation, mastery, and detachment were positively associated with positive affect (joviality, self-assurance, serenity), particularly at Time 2, with some effects persisting to Time 3. Relaxation also negatively predicted certain negative affects (fear, hostility at Time 2). Mastery was associated with positive affect at Time 2 but had limited effects at Time 3. Non-work hassles were associated with higher negative affect (hostility, sadness, fear, fatigue). The seven-factor model of affect fit significantly better than a two-factor model, and CFA fit indices for weekend experience factors were acceptable (e.g., RMSEA=0.09, CFI=0.95). Overall, weekend experiences had stronger immediate (post-weekend) effects than sustained effects into the workweek.
